title: P02 Financial Data Analysis
text:
0.1 Key Findings
This is the analysis report for ex_P02 (Data Science and Finance).
We selected 10 A-share stocks from 7 industries and built a complete pipeline covering data acquisition, cleaning, storage, descriptive statistics, visualization, and CAPM regression.

title: P02 Financial Data Analysis
text:
BYD had the highest annualized return (+30.6%), Vanke the lowest (-32.4%)
All return distributions exhibit fat tails, invalidating the normality assumption
Intra-industry correlations are significantly higher than cross-industry ones
CAPM Beta aligns well with cyclical vs defensive industry classification

title: 2  Data Download
text:
2.3 Download Summary
All 10 stocks downloaded successfully with 1515 trading days each. Both indices (CSI 300 and CSI 500) have 1515 rows. Financial data contains 100 records (10 stocks x 5 years x 2 indicators). All download events are recorded in download_log.txt.

title: 3  Data Cleaning and Storage
text:
3.1 Single-Table Cleaning (6 Steps)
Outlier causes include ex-dividend adjustments in backward-adjusted data, resumption after suspension, and extreme market events.

title: 3  Data Cleaning and Storage
text:
Step
Action
Result




1. Missing detection
Count NaN and empty strings per column
No missing values (baostock data is complete)


2. Missing handling
ffill for prices, fill 0 for volume
Defensive processing, no actual changes


3. Date format
Convert to datetime64[ns]
All 10 stocks converted successfully


4. Data type check
Confirm price/volume columns are numeric
All numeric, no conversion needed


5. Duplicate removal
Deduplicate by date
0 duplicates removed


6. Outlier flagging
Flag daily return exceeding 20% as is_extreme
Flagged but not removed

title: 3  Data Cleaning and Storage
text:
3.2 Wide and Long Table Conversion
Wide table: 1515 days x 10 stocks (one column per stock). Suitable for correlation matrices, multi-line charts, and cross-stock calculations.
Long table: 15,150 rows x 3 columns (date, code, close). Suitable for groupby operations, faceted plots, panel regression, and database storage.

title: 3  Data Cleaning and Storage
text:
3.3 Multi-Table Merge
Stocks (15,150 rows) + Index: left join by date, resulting in 15,150 rows (unchanged, since trading days are aligned).
Monthly macro data (CPI, M2) mapped to daily data via year-month key. CPI has about 1,600 missing values and M2 about 260, both due to publication lag. Final combined dataset: 15,150 rows x 16 columns.

title: 3  Data Cleaning and Storage
text:
3.4 Storage Format Comparison
Base format (Method A): CSV for all data.
Advanced format (Method B): Parquet, chosen for its columnar storage and built-in schema.



Metric
CSV
Parquet




File size
1898.3 KB
888.1 KB (46.8%)


Read time
0.056s
0.022s (40.4%)



Parquet is less than half the size and reads 60% faster. The advantage grows significantly with larger datasets (millions of rows) and selective column reads.

title: 4  Analysis and CAPM Regression
text:
4.1 Descriptive Statistics
Daily log returns: \(r_t = \ln(P_t / P_{t-1})\)
All stocks exhibit leptokurtic (fat-tail) distributions with excess kurtosis well above zero, meaning the normal distribution systematically underestimates extreme risk. ICBC has the lowest volatility (16.3%) and smallest max drawdown (-20.2%), reflecting its defensive nature.

title: 4  Analysis and CAPM Regression
text:
4.2 Visualization Summary
Figure 1 (Normalized Price): BYD surged far above the benchmark; Vanke declined to about 13% of its starting value; banking stocks tracked the CSI 300 closely.
Figure 2 (Return Distribution): All histograms show peaked centers and fat tails relative to the overlaid normal curve.
Figure 3 (Correlation Heatmap): Intra-industry pairs (CMBC-ICBC, Moutai-Wuliangye, PetroChina-Sinopec) show notably higher correlations, supporting diversification across industries.
Figure 4 (M2 vs CSI 300): Weak contemporaneous linear relationship between M2 growth and monthly index returns, likely due to policy transmission lags and counter-cyclical easing.
Figure 5 (ROE Trends): Liquor ROE consistently high; banking ROE stable but declining; Vanke ROE fell sharply; BYD ROE rising.

title: 4  Analysis and CAPM Regression
text:
4.4 Discussion
Q1: Which stocks have Beta > 1? Does this match cyclical vs defensive classification?
Four stocks have Beta > 1: Wuliangye (1.29), BYD (1.28), ZTE (1.24), and Vanke (1.00). These belong to liquor, auto, telecom, and real estate sectors, which are cyclical or growth-oriented. Six stocks have Beta < 1, with ICBC (0.22), Sinopec (0.37), and PetroChina (0.47) being the lowest, belonging to banking and energy, which are classic defensive sectors. The results are highly consistent with the cyclical vs defensive framework.
Q2: Is Alpha significantly different from zero?
Only two stocks show significant alpha at the 5% level. BYD has a positive alpha (p=0.0428), suggesting excess returns beyond CAPM prediction, likely from structural growth in the EV industry. Vanke has a negative alpha (p=0.0083), reflecting sector-specific distress from real estate regulation. The remaining 8 stocks have insignificant alpha, consistent with CAPM theory. Note that CAPM is a single-factor model and significant alpha may stem from omitted factors (size, value).
Q3: Which stock has the highest and lowest R-squared?
Highest: Wuliangye (0.4985). Nearly half of its daily variation is explained by the market, reflecting its status as a large-cap consumer blue chip that moves closely with the market.
Lowest: ICBC (0.0661). Only 6.6% of variation is market-driven. ICBC, as a mega-cap state-owned bank, is driven primarily by banking-specific factors (interest rates, provisions, capital adequacy) rather than overall A-share sentiment. Its extremely low Beta (0.22) corroborates this.

title: 4  Analysis and CAPM Regression
text:
4.5 Conclusions

Industry allocation is the dominant driver of returns during 2020-2026
Fat-tail distributions are universal; VaR models should not rely on normality
Cross-industry diversification effectively reduces idiosyncratic risk
CAPM Beta aligns well with cyclical/defensive industry classification
Significant alpha signals are rare, found only in BYD (+) and Vanke (-)
